data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: TSLA, AMD, SNDK, AMZN
data.description:
The Asian Investor has upgraded Tesla (TSLA) to Buy, citing the recent selloff as an attractive entry point amid the company’s pivot to AI and robotics. Bashar Issa also sees opportunity in Amazon (AMZN), arguing the market is underestimating AWS’s strengthening position in generative AI through Bedrock and its Anthropic partnership.

On the downgrade side, James Foord has moved Sandisk (SNDK) to Hold after the stock surged nearly 20x from its spin-out price, warning of mounting risk factors including NAND cyclicality. The Alpha Analyst has also downgraded Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) from Buy to Hold, pointing to execution risks and a valuation that may have run ahead of fundamentals.

Upgrades

  • Tesla (TSLA): Upgrade to Buy by The Asian Investor. The analyst believes bearish sentiment and a 22% year-to-date decline have created an appealing entry point as Tesla accelerates its transformation toward AI, autonomous driving, and robotics.

    “I believe investors have become too bearish on Tesla’s growth prospects, and I view the current consolidation as an excellent opportunity to increase exposure to Tesla ahead of the first-quarter earnings report.”

  • Amazon.com (AMZN): Buy by Bashar Issa. The analyst argues that AWS is no longer on the defensive in AI, with Bedrock, OpenAI access, and a deepening Anthropic relationship strengthening its competitive position.

    “AMZN is reaping big rewards from its investment in Anthropic, which, through its Claude LLM, has risen through the ranks in the generative AI space. Its capabilities and features have captured attention, particularly from coders and app developers.”

Downgrades

  • Sandisk (SNDK): Downgrade to Hold by James Foord. Following a massive run-up approaching the analyst’s $1,000 price target, mounting risk factors including NAND cyclicality and speculative HBF technology warrant a more cautious stance.

    “NAND is undergoing an upgrade cycle, and this could severely limit pricing in the coming months and years, unless AI demand really expands...While process node improvements could be a headwind for NAND prices, the reality is that most of the capital is still being diverted toward HBM, and that keeps NAND structurally tighter than in previous cycles.”

  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD): Downgrade Buy to Hold by The Alpha Analyst. Despite robust Q4 2025 data center growth, the analyst cites rising execution risks, hyperscaler capex constraints, and weakening valuation support as reasons for caution.

    “AMD is up by almost 18% since the time of my last Buy call, and investors are advised to book some profits or at least await fresh capital deployment. This is an earlier than envisioned downgrade given the extent of upside I saw in November last year for long-term investors. The downgrade from a Buy to Hold acknowledges the change in market regime and its implications on valuations and returns.”

data.title: Brace for World Cup '26 inflation
data.description:
An analysis of the previous nine World Cups showed that hotel prices increased 72% year over year on average, according to Wells Fargo Securities.

This could translate into a 70-basis-point increase in the headline CPI figure, all else equal, according to Wells Fargo analysts.

 

 

 

The 2026 World Cup, which will be held in the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, is estimated to generate $10.9B in revenue, which includes broadcasting rights of over $3B, sponsorships, and ticket sales.

The last cup—the 2022 Qatar World Cup—brought over $7.5B in revenue. In hospitality and ticket sales, the cup brought $769M in revenue.

The 2026 cup is expected to be the most lucrative in history due to expansion to 48 teams, according to FIFA.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Warsh hearing set for next week, Sen. Scott says - report
  • Gas price shock will only partly offset tax refunds - JPMorgan
  • Producer Price Index maintains pace in March but comes below consensus
  • Tuesday’s Economic Calendar
  • IEA head says oil prices will soon reflect severity of Iran crisis

data.title: Leadership shift at Marsh: Mark McGivney appointed as EVP & COO
data.description:
• Marsh (MRSH) announced on Tuesday that CFO Mark McGivney, who has nearly 20 years of experience at the firm, will now take on additional roles as EVP & COO, effective April 15.
  • In this expanded role, he will oversee cross-business strategy and lead inorganic growth (M&A), which shows a clear focus on faster decision-making and execution.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: FB Financial forecasts 2026 net interest margin of 3.7%-3.8% excluding accretion amid elevated competition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FB Financial Corporation (FBK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We reported EPS of $1.10 and an adjusted EPS of $1.12" (President, CEO & Director Christopher Holmes), alongside "net income was $57.5 million or $58.3 million on an adjusted basis" and "pretax preprovision net revenue... was $77.2 million or $78.2 million on an adjusted basis" (President, CEO & Director Holmes).
  • "Revenue declined slightly during the quarter, but expenses have had an even greater decrease to keep our net income and profitability metrics in line with our expectations" (President, CEO & Director Holmes), adding "we were able to grow our pretax preprovision net revenue versus the prior quarter" (President, CEO & Director Holmes).
  • "While we started the year at a slower pace than we originally anticipated, with annualized loan growth of approximately 4% deposit growth around 5%, we are seeing momentum build across the business in the right areas" (COO & CFO Michael Mettee).
  • "During the first quarter, we began to see a more intense wave of competitive pressure, particularly around pricing" (COO & CFO Mettee), while emphasizing "our value proposition is not about being the low-price provider" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • "As bank investors, we watch every basis point of margin" (President, CEO & Director Holmes), and he framed risk readiness as "active and prudent management of our robust capital, robust liquidity and our high reserve levels" (President, CEO & Director Holmes).

Outlook

  • "On a full year basis, we continue to expect both loan and deposit growth in the mid- to high single-digit range, with growth increasingly weighted towards the second half" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • "Based on current conditions, we would expect full year net interest margin excluding loan accretion, to be in the range of 3.7% to 3.8%, representing a modest decline from our prior guidance" (COO & CFO Mettee), and "we would expect second quarter margin to trend towards the lower end of that range before stabilizing" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • Compared with the prior call’s 2026 outlook, management moved from "consistent with where we are today" (COO & CFO Mettee, Q4 2025) to a "modest decline from our prior guidance" (COO & CFO Mettee, Q1 2026) for full-year net interest margin excluding accretion.

Financial Results

  • "Our net interest margin for the quarter was 3.94%" (COO & CFO Mettee) and "total loan yields for the quarter was 6.51%" (COO & CFO Mettee), while "total cost declined to 2.27%" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • "Noninterest income declined $2.4 million during the quarter, primarily driven by lower secondary mortgage volume as well as absence of several nonrecurring items recognized in the prior quarter" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • "First quarter noninterest expense totaled $95.2 million" (COO & CFO Mettee) and "our efficiency ratio for the quarter was 55.2% or 54.3% on an adjusted basis" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • "Our provision expense for the quarter totaled approximately $3 million" (COO & CFO Mettee), "net charge-offs were modest at an annualized rate of 11 basis points" (COO & CFO Mettee), and "our allowance coverage ratio" ended at "1.49% of loans held for investment" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • Capital levels were described as "a very strong position" (COO & CFO Mettee), with "a common equity Tier 1 ratio of 11.5%, a Tier 1 leverage ratio of 10.4% and total risk-based capital of 13.4%" (COO & CFO Mettee).

Q&A

  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co.: asked where competitive pressure is coming from and whether elevated paydowns continue; COO & CFO Mettee said pressure is "generally larger institutions" on loans and "both large and smaller" on deposits, citing "money market rates well above 4" and aggressive CDs; President, CEO & Director Holmes added, "we do expect to continue to see" paydowns, and clarified, "as we're talking about net growth, we're talking about net growth."
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co.: asked about talent disruption; COO & CFO Mettee said "we added... 15 revenue producers in the first quarter" but "also lost a couple" and described it as "offense and defense"; President, CEO & Director Holmes said, "we're looking for the right people at the right time."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: asked why the expense guide implies a pickup; COO & CFO Mettee tied it to "expectation around performance picking up" and "performance-based comp" with "not any expectations of huge like technology investments."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: asked about merger synergy realization and core expense growth; COO & CFO Mettee said the deal landed "pretty much right on top of our deal expense number" and cited "4% to 5% kind of core expense growth" looking toward 2027.
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: pressed on loan cadence and payoffs; COO & CFO Mettee said payoffs and amortization run "$600 million or so" per quarter and "that 500 to 600 range is where I expect" going forward.
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: asked about the NIM guide and rate cuts; COO & CFO Mettee said, "we have a rate cut in our NIM guidance" and pointed to "deposit pressure and thinner loans" as key offsets.
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group: asked about adding specialized businesses and “FirstBank way”; President, CEO & Director Holmes said the community model "will continue" and cited adding "the [ SBA ] line" led by "[ Lane Rads ]."
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group: asked whether M&A is too distracting; President, CEO & Director Holmes said, "The answer for us is no" but added it must be "strategically compelling and financially compelling."
  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: asked where loan demand is improving; COO & CFO Mettee said momentum is "more in operating businesses" and "developing out that strength from a C&I perspective."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: asked about deposit cost direction if rates stay flat; COO & CFO Mettee said new originations were "270" and warned costs could rise to acquire customers, emphasizing, "our goal is always to get the full relationship."
  • Christopher Marinac, Brean Capital: asked if securities can grow NII; COO & CFO Mettee said the portfolio is "about 9% of the balance sheet" and "not top of the list" versus organic loan growth; President, CEO & Director Holmes said the bank is focused on "customer assets and customer deposits" rather than growing wholesale-funded assets.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on competitive pricing, payoffs, margin trajectory, and expense cadence, including detailed follow-ups on "rate cuts" embedded in guidance and quarterly payoffs.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing discipline amid competition, including "we're focused on striking the appropriate balance" (COO & CFO Mettee) and that margin pressure is expected as "competitive dynamics remain elevated" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • Versus last quarter: management tone remained optimistic on growth and disruption-driven opportunity, but guidance language sharpened on margin, shifting from "consistent with where we are today" (COO & CFO Mettee, Q4 2025) to a "modest decline from our prior guidance" (COO & CFO Mettee, Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance: Q1 2026 introduced a lower full-year NIM outlook excluding accretion at "3.7% to 3.8%" (COO & CFO Mettee) versus Q4 2025 guidance of "between 3.78% and 3.83%" for 2026 (COO & CFO Mettee, Q4 2025).
  • Strategy: Q1 2026 placed new emphasis on adding specialized verticals, with President, CEO & Director Holmes citing the added "[ SBA ] line"; Q4 2025 emphasized post-acquisition focus on "cement our focus on the customer" (President, CEO & Director Holmes, Q4 2025).
  • Analyst focus: Q1 2026 questions concentrated on pricing competition, payoffs, and margin sensitivity; Q4 2025 leaned more into disruption-driven hiring, deposit beta, and framing the M&A environment.

Risks and Concerns

  • "A more intense wave of competitive pressure, particularly around pricing" (COO & CFO Mettee) and expected continued "pressure on margin" (COO & CFO Mettee) were framed as near-term headwinds.
  • Mortgage cyclicality and volatility were highlighted as production "slowed as the quarter progressed due to the increased interest rate volatility and heightened uncertainty in the housing market" (COO & CFO Mettee).
  • Credit and macro monitoring included specific reference to "potential macroeconomic events stemming from the conflict in the Middle East" (COO & CFO Mettee), though management said exposure to sensitive industries is "minimal" (COO & CFO Mettee).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as a quarter where profitability held as expenses fell faster than revenue, while competitive pricing and deposit dynamics intensified. The company reiterated its mid- to high single-digit loan and deposit growth expectation but shifted its full-year net interest margin outlook (excluding accretion) down to 3.7%-3.8%, citing continued competitive pressure. Executives also described active talent recruiting and early efforts to broaden specialized lending—highlighting a newly built SBA capability—while maintaining an emphasis on relationship-driven growth and capital flexibility, including opportunistic share repurchases during market volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street advances for a second day as investors hope for a U.S.-Iran peace deal
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were comfortably in the green on Tuesday as investors grew optimistic about potential U.S.-Iran peace talks that could ease tensions in the Middle East.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.8%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.5%.

The gains followed comments from President Donald Trump indicating that Iran had reached out for potential negotiations, even as the U.S. moved forward with a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Asian equities also rallied on hopes that a deal could lower oil prices and support global growth.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower at 4.28%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was unchanged at 3.78%.

On the economic front, the March U.S. Producer Price Index came in at +0.5% MoM vs. +1.2% consensus and +0.5% prior (revised from +0.7%), and +4.0% YoY vs. +4.7% consensus and +3.4% prior.

Core PPI, which excludes foods and energy, was +0.1% MoM vs. +0.5% consensus and +0.3% prior (revised from +0.5%), and +3.8% YoY vs. +4.2% consensus and +3.8% prior (revised from +3.9%).

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were lower at $93.9, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $96.4 per barrel.

data.title: Samsung hikes prices on smartphones and tablets as Apple holds steady
data.description:
Samsung (SSNLF) has recently hiked up the price on several of its smartphones and tablets in the U.S. market, while Apple's latest iPhone 17 models have held steady.

Samsung's Galaxy Flip 7 with 512GB of storage has increased to $1,299 from $1,219 in the U.S. over the past week, in a move observed by PhoneArena. The Galaxy S25 FE 256GB rose to $749 from $709, and the Galaxy S25 Edge 512GB climbed to $1,299 from $1,219.

The Galaxy Z Fold 7 1TB also increased to $2,499 from $2,419. However, its entry-level 256G remained at $1,999.

The price hikes are likely linked to rising memory costs as the AI data center buildout continues to take precedence when it comes to supply.

In contrast, Apple has not increased the sticker price on its iPhone 17 lineup. The iPhone 17 Pro's starting price remains unchanged at $1,099. The iPhone 17 Air remains at $999, and the iPhone 17 base model still starts at $799. Apple also introduced the iPhone 17e that starts at $599. 

Samsung also edged up its prices on an array of its tablets. The Galaxy Tab S11, S11 Ultra, S10 FE, S10 Lite, S10 Plus, and A11 Plus have all increased in price by at least $50. The S11 Ultra featured the most prominent increases, with the 1TB going to $1,899 from $1,619.

Apple might increase the price of its upcoming iPhone 18 models due in part to the introduction of more powerful chips. The iPhone 17 Pro was priced $100 more than the iPhone 16 Pro. However, the iPhone 17 base model remained the same as the iPhone 16 base model when it was released.

data.title: Meta's CEO Mark Zuckerberg moves desk to AI lab, sharpening focus: report
data.description:
Meta Platforms' (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg has moved his desk to the AI lab and is coding all day, Semafor reported, citing the company's President and Vice Chairman Dina McCormick.

Zuckerberg has “actually moved his desk and is seated in the AI lab … and he’s coding all day long,” McCormick told Semafor World Economy on Tuesday, the report added.

"I think he feels so strongly that he has to understand it at that level to really think about how to make our model the strongest it can be,"  said McCormick, who joined Meta in January.

Meta did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

McCormick also said that she had to transition from the pace of her former roles — ranging from Goldman Sachs to Deputy National Security Advisor for Strategy under President Donald Trump — to the tech giant's breakneck speed, the report noted.

"Silicon Valley, or at least I’ve seen Meta move much, much more quickly than when I worked in government or even on Wall Street," said McCormick.

When asked about the recent rulings on Meta’s legal liability for harm to teen mental health, McCormick noted that, "We disagree respectfully with those verdicts, and we’re appealing."

McCormick added that "I have seen how seriously the leadership of the company takes this to ensure that there is not harmful content, and most importantly, to ensure that parents are empowered."

Last week, Meta unveiled Muse Spark, the first AI model developed by the company's Superintelligence team. Downloads of Meta's AI app on iOS in the U.S. saw an increase of 87% day-over-day on April 8, when the company released Muse Spark, according to market intelligence firm Sensor Tower.

data.title: FCC will have “minor” role in Paramount/Warner Bros. merger, looking into NFL deals, The View
data.description:
The Federal Communications Commission expects to have “a minor, if any, role” in Paramount Skydance’s (PSKY) acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and will likely only look into the foreign involvement in the deal.

In an interview on CNBC, FCC Commissioner Brendan Carr said that although the deal involves the ownership of CBS, the agency will not be very involved in the regulatory process. While there are some issues regarding the investment of foreign debt, that will likely be the only role the FCC will take.

During the interview, Carr disclosed that his agency has opened an investigation into Disney’s (DIS) “The View” and any violations of FCC rules pertaining to conflicts of interest and allowing equal time for candidates on broadcast TV.

“We will enforce ‘equal time’ rules,” Carr stressed.

“This is more about disclosure…allowing for more speech, not less,” Carr said, adding that if “broadcasters don’t want to comply with their public interest obligation, they can switch to other mediums like streaming and cable.”

When asked about the FCC's stance on partnerships between the National Football League and streaming channels, Carr acknowledged his agency is investigating these deals as they pertain to the “health of broadcasters.”

“The NFL has reached a tipping point where they are putting too many games behind a paywall,” encouraging the FCC to assist the Department of Justice in their antitrust investigation of the league, he noted.

data.title: CME adds Eris SOFR swap options for managing US dollar interest rate risk
data.description:
CME Group (CME) will launch options on Eris SOFR swap futures on June 16, 2026, providing investors with a way to manage U.S. dollar interest rate risk more precisely, the company said Tuesday. The introduction is subject to pending regulatory review.

"Eris SOFR swap options will complement our existing interest rate product suite, which delivers deep liquidity, price transparency, and unmatched margin efficiencies to global market participants," said Agha Mirza, CME Group global head of Rates and OTC Products.

Eris SOFR Swap futures replicate interest rate swap cash flows, offering the standardization and capital savings of exchange-traded instruments, CME said. Adding options on two-year, five-year, and 10-year Eris SOFR Swap futures will support more sophisticated hedging strategies, such as managing non-linear risk in mortgage-backed portfolios, it added.

Michael Riddle, CEO of Eris Innovations, commented, "By mirroring the structure of forward-premium OTC swaptions, Eris SOFR Swap options can deliver cost optimization, margin efficiencies, and trading simplicity amid shifting economic conditions."

The new options add to CME Group's (CME) interest rate market offerings, spanning futures and options for a broad range of benchmark products, including U.S. Treasuries, SOFR, Fed Funds,TBAs, and credit, among others. Its U.S. Treasury and SOFR contracts trade side-by-side on the CME Globex platform with BrokerTec cash securities.

data.title: Oil tanker linked to China made U-turn as ships test U.S. naval blockade of Iran - report
data.description:
A U.S.-sanctioned tanker linked to China that was being watched as one of the first vessels to test President Trump's naval blockade apparently has U-turned after sailing out of the Strait of Hormuz and into the Gulf of Oman, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

The vessel Rich Starry was blacklisted by the U.S. in 2023 for helping Iran evade energy sanctions and has a history of spoofing its satellite signals, according to TankerTrackers.com, which monitors vessels using satellite data.

Another tanker, the Elpis, was in the strait and headed into the Gulf of Oman just as the blockade began; ship-tracking platforms Kpler and Vortexa indicate the vessel docked at an Iranian port in the gulf before attempting to pass through Hormuz, meaning it could be a potential target for the U.S. naval patrol.

The Elpis paused its journey at roughly the same location that Rich Starry turned around, although it has not broadcast a signal for several hours, meaning it could have carried on its journey or turned back.

China called the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports a "dangerous and irresponsible" move.

Benchmark crude oil futures fell sharply on Tuesday as signs of ​a possible resumption in talks to end the Iran war eased supply concerns stemming ‌from the blockade.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery down 5.9% to $93.23/bbl and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery down 4.4% to $88.86/bbl.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI)
